summary: Gruber announces the shutdown of Vesper, the notes app he built with Brent Simmons and Dave Wiskus through their company Q Branch. The app never generated enough revenue to sustain development, despite being well-designed and relatively popular. Gruber argues their fundamental mistake was building for iPhone first instead of Mac, where paid apps command higher prices, and that they should have eventually adopted a subscription model with free apps and paid sync. He reflects on the broader economics of the iOS App Store, where free apps dominate and paid productivity apps struggle to gain traction. The post is both a postmortem and a farewell, mixing business analysis with genuine emotional attachment to the product and the team.
key_points: Vesper failed because it never made enough money as a paid iOS app in a market dominated by free alternatives
The correct strategy would have been to ship the Mac version first at $20, then use that revenue to fund iOS development
A subscription model with free apps and paid sync (~$15/year) would have been more sustainable than upfront app purchases
The iOS 7 redesign forced them to essentially build the iPhone app twice, wasting critical development time
The chicken-and-egg problem of needing cross-platform availability to attract users while needing revenue to fund cross-platform development was never solved
money_quotes: What went wrong was very simple. We never made enough money.
In my career, the only things I've done that I'm prouder of are writing Daring Fireball and the creation of Markdown.
The market for paid productivity apps for iOS is simply too difficult.
What most people do when looking for, say, an iPhone notes app, is search the App Store for "notes", and then start downloading free apps until they find one that seems good enough.
The number one reason, by a long shot, that people didn't buy Vesper is because it wasn't available for the Mac.
I'm a firm believer that you always need some good luck to succeed.
Any app you intend to bring to mobile should be designed for mobile first.
With "Vesper" we were thinking things like beautiful, smart, clever, strong. In the end, the name was more apt than we knew, because it also carries heartbreak.
key_insight: Building a paid iOS app first was the fatal mistake — the Mac's healthier paid software market should have funded the path to cross-platform, and ultimately a subscription model was the only sustainable answer.
